text: Most glorious of immortals, Zeus, invoked by many names, ever almighty, First Cause of Nature, ruling all things by law—hail to thee. For it is right that all mortals should address thee, since we are thy offspring and alone of all living things that move upon the earth have received the gift of speech in thy likeness.
source: Hymn to Zeus, lines 1–6

text: This whole universe, as it wheels around the earth, obeys thee wheresoever thou dost guide it, and is willingly governed by thee. Such a minister hast thou in thine invincible hands—the two-edged, blazing, ever-living thunderbolt.
source: Hymn to Zeus, lines 7–11

text: Under its stroke all nature shudders; by it thou guidest the universal Reason—that common light which mingles with the great and the lesser lights of heaven.
source: Hymn to Zeus, lines 12–14

text: Thou hast so welded into one all good things with all evil that there arises out of them together one everlasting Reason, which those who are evil flee from and ignore.
source: Hymn to Zeus, lines 15–18

text: No work is wrought upon the earth apart from thee, O God, neither in the divine ethereal vault of heaven, nor in the depths of the sea, save only the deeds which wicked men do in their folly.
source: Hymn to Zeus, lines 19–21

text: Thou knowest how to make the crooked straight, to bring order out of chaos; things unlovely are lovely to thee; for so thou hast fitted together all good things and all evil, that there reigns one everlasting Reason over all.
source: Hymn to Zeus, lines 22–25

text: Evil men flee from thy Law, wretched, and knowing not the universal good, rush blindly each after his own goal—some in vainglorious strife, others in disordered pursuit of gain, others in softness and the pleasures of the flesh.
source: Hymn to Zeus, lines 26–30

text: But thou, O Zeus, giver of all, shrouded in dark clouds and wielding the vivid lightning, rescue mankind from its painful ignorance. Scatter it from our souls and grant us to win the wisdom by which thou governest all things with justice.
source: Hymn to Zeus, lines 31–35

text: So that, being honoured, we may render honour to thee in turn, singing thy works unceasingly, as is fitting for a mortal; since neither mortals nor gods have any greater privilege than this—to sing for ever in righteousness the praises of the universal Law.
source: Hymn to Zeus, lines 36–39

text: Lead me, O Zeus, and thou, O Fate, wherever you have ordained for me. I will follow without faltering; and even if I were unwilling and shrank from it, a wretch, I should have to follow all the same.
source: Fragment, via Epictetus Enchiridion Ch. 53 / Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.33

text: The goal of life is to live in agreement with nature, which means to live according to virtue; for nature directs us toward virtue.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.87

text: Virtue is sufficient by itself for happiness. Whatever else there may be—wealth, reputation, pleasure—none of it is truly good unless virtue is present to make use of it.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.127

text: The wise man is not disturbed by fortune. He uses good fortune with moderation and faces ill fortune with courage; for all of it he converts into the material of virtue.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.117

text: The things which are truly good are of the soul: wisdom, justice, courage, self-control. The things of the body and of fortune are neither good nor evil, but indifferent—they may be preferred or dispreferred, yet they do not determine a man's happiness.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.102

text: The universal Reason pervades all matter as honey pervades the honeycomb; it holds together and governs everything, from the greatest bodies of the heavens down to the smallest things upon the earth.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.138

text: God is one, and is called by many names according to his many powers: Zeus, because he is the cause of life; Athena, because his ruling faculty extends into the ether; Hephaestus, because it extends into fire. The wise man sees the one beneath the many.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.135

text: Fate is the Reason by which the world is governed; or it is the providence and forethought of God directing all things to their appointed end. Submit to it, and you live well.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.149

text: The emotions are not accidents that fall upon the wise man from without; they arise from false judgements about things. Correct the judgement, and you correct the passion.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.110

text: There is one world, one universal nature, one Law, one Reason governing all. To live in harmony with this common Reason is what it means to live well and to live justly.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.88

text: If you want to be praised by those who come after you, care for those who live with you now. It is the same justice which binds us to the living that will make us remembered by the dead.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.26

text: Persevere; this is the beginning of wisdom. Most men begin but do not continue; yet it is only by continuance that philosophy purges the soul and forms the habit of virtue.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.170

text: The good is that which completes and fulfils our nature as rational beings; the evil is that which corrupts it. All else—pleasure, pain, wealth, poverty—is neither the one nor the other.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.94
